text: When you were a kid, a single summer felt like it lasted forever.
scene: A tiny stick kid lies back in tall green grass under a huge slow yellow sun, totally relaxed, with the word SUMMER hand-lettered very wide and stretched across the top. Lazy and endless. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the big yellow sun.

text: One school year was an entire era.
scene: A tiny stick kid stands at the bottom of a single giant calendar page that towers up impossibly tall, labeled SCHOOL YEAR, stretching way up out of frame. One year feels enormous. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the towering calendar.

text: Now you blink, and somehow it is December again.
scene: An adult stick figure blinks as a wall calendar's pages blast off and fly away in a fast blur, big hand-lettered DECEMBER. Fast and gone. Selective pop: red motion lines.

text: A whole year, gone in what felt like a couple of weeks.
scene: A whole year of calendar pages, all flown off and crushed into one tiny little paper ball in a stick figure's open hand. A year shrunk to nothing. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the tiny paper ball.

text: And here's the strange part.
scene: A stick figure stops and tilts its head, one big red question mark floating over it. The strange part. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the red question mark.

text: The year did not actually change.
scene: A plain wall calendar sitting perfectly still and unchanged with a small green check on it, totally normal. Nothing actually changed. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the green check.

text: It is still exactly 365 days, the same as when you were 8.
scene: One big bold calendar with the number 365 hand-lettered large across it, rock solid and unchanged, a small AGE 8 tag and a NOW tag both pinned to it. The number never moved. Selective pop: the number 365.

text: So why does it keep going faster?
scene: A wall clock with its hands spinning fast in a blur with red speed lines, a big red question mark beside it. Why faster? Mostly empty. Selective pop: the red speed lines.

text: And why does it keep getting worse every single year?
scene: A left-to-right row of identical clocks, each one spinning a little faster than the last, red speed lines growing. Speeding up every year. Selective pop: the red speed lines on the fastest clock.

text: The answer has almost nothing to do with time.
scene: A big wall clock with a bold red X struck across it. The answer is not about time. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the red X.

text: It is about what your brain bothers to remember, and what it quietly throws away.
scene: A doodle brain sorting little memory cards: a few glowing ones kept in a box, the dull rest tossed into a trash bin. Keep the new, toss the rest. Selective pop: the glowing kept cards.

text: Everyone feels this, and it is weirdly consistent.
scene: A row of stick figures of different ages, kid to old, all nodding yes in unison, a single shared thought bubble of a fast-spinning clock above them. Everyone feels it. Selective pop: the clock in the bubble.

text: Ask a 10 year old how long until summer break, and it sounds like forever away.
scene: A little stick kid stands at the start of an extremely long road that shrinks to a tiny far-off sign labeled SUMMER, impossibly far away. Forever away. Selective pop: the distant SUMMER sign.

text: Ask a 40 year old where the year went and they honestly cannot tell you.
scene: A stick adult shrugs with palms up at a totally blank wall calendar, a red question mark over their head. Where did it go? Mostly empty. Selective pop: the red question mark.

text: It is not your imagination, and it is not just you.
scene: A grid of identical little shrugging stick figures filling the frame, all exactly the same. It is everyone, not just you. Selective pop: subtle, one figure faintly outlined.

text: Time really does seem to speed up steadily for your whole life.
scene: A simple left-to-right life line with a clock on it that gets bigger and spins faster as it moves right. A steady speed-up across a whole life. Selective pop: red speed lines.

text: The clock never changes, the feeling always does.
scene: A rock-steady clock on the left, and on the right a wobbly speeding-up arrow hand-lettered FEELING. The clock is fixed, the feeling moves. Selective pop: the red FEELING arrow.

text: Now, there is an old answer to this that sounds perfect.
scene: A neat, tidy pie chart drawn cleanly with a little sparkle, looking perfect and obvious. The old answer. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the clean blue pie slice.

text: When you are five, one year is a fifth of your entire life, a huge slice.
scene: A pie chart cut into five parts with one huge slice boldly shaded and labeled ONE YEAR, a tiny stick kid beside it. A big chunk of a small life. Selective pop: the big shaded slice.

text: When you are 50, that same year is just one fiftieth, a sliver.
scene: A pie chart cut into fifty thin parts with one razor-thin sliver shaded and labeled ONE YEAR, a stick adult beside it. A tiny sliver of a big life. Selective pop: the thin red sliver.

text: So each year feels smaller because it is a smaller piece of everything you have
scene: A left-to-right row of pie slices getting thinner and thinner, each year a smaller piece. Each year shrinks. Selective pop: the shrinking slices.

text: lived, clean, tidy, and mostly wrong.
scene: The neat pie chart with a big red X slammed across it and hand-lettered MOSTLY WRONG. Tidy, but wrong. Selective pop: the red X.

text: Because if that were really it, time would just feel like it's,
scene: A perfectly smooth gentle downhill ramp line with a small marble rolling steadily down it. If it were true, it would feel smooth. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the blue ramp.

text: it shrinks smoothly year after year.
scene: The same smooth ramp, now with little even tick marks for each year, all identical spacing. Smooth and even. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the even ticks.

text: But that is not how it feels.
scene: The smooth ramp suddenly cracks into a jagged uneven jumble of steps, some tall, some flat. Not smooth at all. Selective pop: the red jagged break.

text: It feels like some years vanish and a few somehow stretch out.
scene: A row of year blocks, most squished to thin slivers but a couple stretched huge and wide. Some vanish, some stretch. Selective pop: the one big stretched block.

text: The real reason is hiding somewhere you would not think to look, your memory.
scene: A doodle brain with a little spotlight on it and a red arrow pointing to it, hand-lettered MEMORY. The real reason. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the brain and the red arrow.

text: Here is what your brain is actually doing.
scene: A doodle brain with little gears turning busily inside it. Here is what it does. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the turning gears.

text: It does not measure time with a clock.
scene: A doodle brain beside a wall clock with a bold red X over the clock. The brain does not use a clock. Selective pop: the red X.

text: When you look back and ask how long something took,
scene: A stick figure looking back over its shoulder at a trail of little footprints behind it, wondering. Looking back. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the trail.

text: your brain quietly counts how many distinct memories it made in that stretch.
scene: A doodle brain counting a stack of little glowing polaroid photos like counting money, hand-lettered MEMORIES = TIME. It counts memories. Selective pop: the glowing polaroids.

text: A lot of new vivid memories and the time feels long.
scene: A big tall stack of bright glowing polaroids next to a long stretched-out time bar. Many memories, long time. Selective pop: the glowing stack.

text: Almost no memories and it feels like it barely happened at all.
scene: A nearly empty shelf with just one faint gray polaroid next to a tiny short time bar. Few memories, barely any time. Selective pop: the lone faint photo.

text: Looking backward, time is not made of hours, it is made of memories.
scene: A wall clock dissolving and turning into a flurry of little glowing polaroids. Time is made of memories, not hours. Selective pop: the glowing photos.

text: And memories are made of new things.
scene: A single glowing polaroid with a bright NEW starburst on it. Memories are made of new. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the NEW star.

text: So picture being a kid again, almost everything is a first.
scene: A little stick kid standing wide-eyed and amazed, surrounded by glowing little 1ST badges floating all around. Everything is a first. Selective pop: the glowing 1ST badges.

text: First day of school, first bike without training wheels,
scene: Two bright glowing polaroids: a little school building labeled 1ST SCHOOL and a wobbly bike labeled 1ST BIKE, both vivid. Firsts, recorded bright. Selective pop: the glowing polaroids.

text: first time you saw the ocean, first friend, first concert,
scene: A cluster of bright glowing polaroids: a big wave labeled OCEAN, two stick figures labeled FRIEND, a tiny stage labeled CONCERT. Packed with firsts. Selective pop: the glowing photos.

text: first heartbreak.
scene: One glowing polaroid with a simple little cracked heart on it, labeled 1ST. Even this one, recorded in full. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the red heart.

text: Your brain is recording constantly in high detail because all of it is brand new
scene: A doodle brain with a little camera lens snapping photos rapidly, flash going off, lots of click lines. Recording constantly. Selective pop: the camera flash.

text: and it does not want to miss anything.
scene: A doodle brain holding a butterfly net, eagerly catching little glowing moments out of the air. Does not want to miss anything. Selective pop: the glowing moments.

text: That is why one childhood summer feels enormous when you look back.
scene: A tiny stick kid dwarfed beside an enormous towering glowing block labeled SUMMER, packed full of little photos. One summer, enormous. Selective pop: the giant glowing block.

text: It was not longer than your summers now.
scene: Two identical-size calendar blocks side by side, one labeled KID SUMMER, one labeled NOW, exactly equal. Same length really. Selective pop: both blocks equal.

text: It was just absolutely packed with markers.
scene: A summer block absolutely crammed full of little glowing polaroids, overflowing the edges. Packed with markers. Selective pop: the overflowing glowing photos.

text: It was fuller.
scene: Two jars side by side, one stuffed completely full of glowing marbles labeled KID, one nearly empty labeled NOW. Fuller. Selective pop: the full glowing jar.

text: And then you grow up and life slowly turns into a routine.
scene: A stick figure stepping onto a long gray treadmill that stretches off flat and endless. Life turns to routine. Selective pop: the gray treadmill.

text: The same drive to work, the same week on repeat,
scene: A row of identical gray boxes all labeled SAME, lined up, dull and repeating. The same week on repeat. Selective pop: subtle, flat gray.

text: same faces, same lunch, same scroll before bed.
scene: A grid of identical little gray icons repeating: same face, same sandwich, same phone, over and over. All the same. Selective pop: subtle, flat gray grid.

text: And your brain, which is efficient and a little lazy,
scene: A doodle brain lounging back in a tiny lawn chair, half asleep, relaxed and lazy. Efficient and a little lazy. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the brain.

text: stops bothering to write down what it has already seen a thousand times.
scene: A doodle brain setting down its pencil and waving away an identical gray photo it has already seen. Stops writing it down. Selective pop: the discarded gray photo.

text: It compresses the repeats into almost nothing.
scene: A tall stack of identical gray boxes all labeled TUESDAY being squashed flat into one thin little line. Compressed to almost nothing. Selective pop: the thin flat line.

text: A whole month of ordinary Tuesdays leaves barely a trace.
scene: A month calendar grid, every single day gray and identical, leaving just one tiny faint pencil dot. Barely a trace. Selective pop: the single faint dot.

text: So when you look back at the year and ask where it went,
scene: A stick figure peering into a big open box labeled THIS YEAR, finding it nearly empty inside. Where did it go? Selective pop: the empty box.

text: there's almost nothing recorded there to measure.
scene: A film reel unspooled to show the tape is almost completely blank, just a few frames. Nothing recorded. Selective pop: the blank film.

text: And your brain just shrugs and says, huh, that went fast.
scene: A doodle brain shrugging with little stick arms up, a small speech bubble saying that went fast. The brain just shrugs. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the brain.

text: The time did not speed up.
scene: A single calm wall clock ticking steadily and normally, totally unbothered. Time did not change. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the steady clock.

text: You simply stopped saving the file.
scene: A floppy-disk SAVE icon with a bold red X over it, a little unsaved file fading away. You stopped saving the file. Selective pop: the red X.

text: A few scientists think there may be a second layer to it.
scene: Two little stick scientists in lab coats pointing at a brain with a small red question mark between them. A debated second idea. Selective pop: the red question mark.

text: That as the brain gets bigger and it's wiring gets longer,
scene: A doodle brain growing larger with long winding wires stretching out longer and longer. Bigger brain, longer wiring. Selective pop: the long wires.

text: the signals travel a little slower.
scene: A little signal dot crawling slowly along a very long winding wire with a tiny snail beside it. Signals travel slower. Selective pop: the snail.

text: So you actually take in fewer fresh mental snapshots per second than a child does.
scene: On the left a kid's eye firing out many little snapshots in a bright burst; on the right an adult eye firing just a few, slowly. Fewer snapshots per second. Selective pop: the kid's bright burst.

text: Fewer frames, faster film.
scene: A film strip with only a few frames on it whizzing through a projector fast and blurry. Fewer frames, faster film. Selective pop: red speed blur.

text: That part is still up for debate.
scene: A single big red question mark with a small DEBATED tag, two tiny stick scientists shrugging at each other beneath it. Still debated. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the red question mark.

text: But the memory piece, the part about routine, almost everyone agrees on.
scene: A doodle brain labeled MEMORY with a big green check on it, a row of little stick figures all nodding in agreement. Everyone agrees on this part. Selective pop: the green check.

text: So here's the quiet truth sitting under all of it.
scene: A single small glowing lightbulb sitting calmly in the middle of a clean empty frame, quiet and steady. The quiet truth. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the soft glow.

text: Time is not actually accelerating.
scene: A calm wall clock ticking at a perfectly normal steady pace, no blur, totally serene. Not accelerating. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the calm clock.

text: Your life is not slipping away faster than it used to.
scene: A stick figure standing calm and grounded with a gentle green reassuring check over it, nothing rushing or draining. Steady, not slipping. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the green check.

text: Your brain is just writing less of it down.
scene: An open journal: a couple of pages dense with little doodles, the rest of the pages totally blank, a calm hand resting on it. Writing less down. Selective pop: the few inked pages.

text: Because so much of it now looks like something it has already seen before.
scene: A stick figure flipping through a photo album where every photo is the exact same gray picture, over and over. Already seen it. Selective pop: subtle, repeated gray.

text: The years do not feel short because they were empty.
scene: A jar labeled THE YEAR, completely full, but full of identical gray marbles all the same. Not empty. Selective pop: the gray marbles.

text: They feel short because they were full of the same thing over and over.
scene: The same open journal, most pages blank, hand-lettered NOT GONE, JUST BLANK, a calm hand resting. Full of the same thing. Selective pop: subtle, the inked pages.

text: And your brain only keeps what is new.
scene: A doodle brain holding up one bright glowing NEW photo and letting a pile of identical gray ones fall away. Keeps only what is new. Selective pop: the glowing NEW photo.

text: And that is the good part.
scene: A small stick figure looking up at a bright rising sun with a little smile, hopeful and light. The good part. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the bright sun.

text: Because it means the speed is not locked in.
scene: A speed dial with a padlock on it springing open, the lock popped off. Not locked in. Selective pop: the green open lock.

text: You can slow it back down.
scene: A big hand turning a speed dial back down from FAST toward SLOW. You can slow it down. Selective pop: the green SLOW end of the dial.

text: Not with a clock. With newness.
scene: A wall clock with a red X beside a bright glowing star hand-lettered NEW. Not a clock, newness. Selective pop: the glowing NEW star.

text: A trip somewhere you have never been.
scene: A stick figure with a little suitcase stepping toward a bright glowing horizon with mountains it has never seen. A new place. Selective pop: the glowing horizon.

text: A skill you are genuinely bad at.
scene: A stick figure clumsily trying to juggle, balls dropping everywhere, but a bright glowing 1ST badge over it. A new skill you are bad at. Selective pop: the glowing 1ST badge.

text: A different way home.
scene: A simple fork in a path: one plain gray straight arrow, one curvy path lit with little glowing dots. A different way home. Selective pop: the glowing curvy path.

text: Anything your brain is forced to actually pay attention to,
scene: A doodle brain suddenly wide awake with big alert eyes, fully paying attention. Forced to pay attention. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the alert brain.

text: it records in full and recorded time feels long when you look back on it.
scene: A doodle brain snapping one big bright detailed photo, with a long stretched-out glowing time bar beside it. Recorded full, feels long. Selective pop: the glowing long bar.

text: It is why one week in a country you have never seen
scene: One huge bright glowing polaroid hand-lettered ONE WEEK SOMEWHERE NEW, packed with vivid little details. Selective pop: the big glowing polaroid.

text: can feel bigger in memory than three flat months of normal life.
scene: The one huge glowing ONE WEEK polaroid towering over three tiny thin gray bars labeled 3 MONTHS SAME. The big one dwarfs them. Selective pop: the glowing week versus the gray bars.

text: The people whose years feel rich and slow are not younger than you.
scene: An older stick figure smiling, holding a thick photo album bursting with bright glowing photos. Rich and slow, not younger. Selective pop: the glowing album.

text: They are just collecting more firsts.
scene: A stick figure happily dropping bright glowing 1ST badges into a big jar, collecting them. Collecting more firsts. Selective pop: the glowing 1ST badges.

text: So if it feels like the years are flying past,
scene: Calendar pages flying off the wall in a fast blur again, a stick figure watching calmly this time. Years flying past. Selective pop: the red motion lines.

text: the trick was never to find more time.
scene: A pile of clocks with a big red X over them, hand-lettered NOT MORE TIME. The trick is not more time. Selective pop: the red X.

text: It is to give your brain more that is new.
scene: A doodle brain happily receiving a bright glowing gift box labeled NEW. Give your brain more new. Selective pop: the glowing NEW gift.

text: Take the long way home.
scene: A stick figure choosing the curvy long path lit with little glowing dots over the plain straight gray one. Take the long way. Selective pop: the glowing path.

text: Learn the thing you are bad at.
scene: A stick figure wobbling on a unicycle for the first time, arms flailing, a bright glowing 1ST over it, grinning. Learn the thing you are bad at. Selective pop: the glowing 1ST.

text: Every first you hand yourself is one more piece of your life
scene: A stick figure placing one bright glowing puzzle piece into a big picture of its own life. One more piece. Selective pop: the glowing puzzle piece.

text: you will actually remember living.
scene: A stick figure looking back at a long trail of bright glowing photos stretching behind it, full and vivid. Actually remember living. Selective pop: the glowing trail.

text: Time was never the thing speeding up.
scene: A single calm steady clock ticking normally and peaceful, with a small green check beside it. Time was never the thing. Mostly empty. Selective pop: the green check.

text: You just have a lot more say in it than you thought.
scene: A confident stick figure stepping forward toward a bright glowing doorway labeled NEW, one hand on a big dial turned up. You have a say. Selective pop: the glowing doorway.
